text: {name}: "Clamps away. Let's see what's out there."
{name} raps the bulkhead twice for luck before the burn.
{name}: "Another dock behind us. They all start looking the same."
{name} watches the port shrink to a pinprick and doesn't say anything for a while.
{name}: "Finally. I was starting to grow roots back there."
{name} eases her into the lane like threading a needle. "She flies clean today, Captain."
{name}: "Solid ground. Well — solid deck plating. Close enough."
{name} is already eyeing the market boards. "Reckon there's coin here."
{name} clocks every exit off the dock before stepping down the ramp.
{name}: "First round's on the captain, right? Right."
{name} goes quiet at the sight of the dock crèche. Old memories travel light.
{name} plays solitaire against the drive hum and loses, somehow.
{name} stares at the black a long while. "It looks back, you know. If you let it."
{name} murmurs a small grace over nothing in particular.
{name} tells a story about {origin} that you're fairly sure changes every time.
{name} leaves a mug of something warm on the arm of your chair without a word.
{name}: "Peace and quiet. That's how you know something's about to go wrong."
{name}: "Somewhere out past the charts there's a shore nobody's named yet. I'd like to name one."
{name} is doing something ambitious with the ration paste. It smells almost like food.
{name} has a panel off again, elbow-deep in the guts of her, whispering to the wiring.
{name} catches your eye across the galley and just nods. You've been through some things.
{name} eats alone, back to the bulkhead, and doesn't look up when you pass.
{name} runs a hand along the console. "She's got a pull to port on cold starts. I've stopped fighting it."
{name} is inventorying the medkit for the third time this week. Some habits are load-bearing.
{name} strips the sidearm down and back up again without looking, humming something tuneless.
{name} has the ledger open and is muttering numbers under their breath like a rosary.
{name} straightens something on the console that was already straight.
{name} does mental math on what the current cargo would fetch at three different ports, unprompted.
{name}: "Whatever's next, Captain, count me in. Same as always."
{name} says nothing for a long while, which from {name} counts as a whole conversation.
{name}: "Everyone strap in — this is going to get loud!"
{name} is grinning. That's never comforting. "Let's give 'em a fight to remember."
{name}: "We do NOT have to win this. We just have to still be flying after."
{name} racks the weapons console. "Say the word, Captain."
{name} kisses two fingers and touches the console. "Watch over this old bucket."
{name} lets out a breath they'd clearly been holding for a while.
{name}: "THAT'S what I'm talking about! Anyone else? No? Didn't think so."
{name} watches the wreck tumble and doesn't celebrate. "Somebody's not coming home from that."
{name} pats the weapons console like a good dog. "Never doubted you."
{name}: "Running's not losing. Running's living. Punch it!"
{name} spits. "I'll remember their registry. We don't run twice from the same ship."
{name} keeps their hands visible and their mouth shut while the cutter runs the registry.
{name}: "Union scan. Smile pretty. They love that."
{name} is already crawling toward the noise with a wrench in their teeth.
{name}: "I've got it, I've got it — do NOT touch anything, Captain."
{name}: "She only breaks when you say she's running well. Who said it? WHO said it?"
{name} braces in the hatchway as the hull sings under the impacts.
{name} threads the swarm with small, sure corrections. "Easy. Easy. There."
{name}: "One captain's junk, another captain's payday. Grab it."
{name} is halfway into the wreck before the netting's even secured.
{name} helps the survivors aboard, quiet and careful, like they've been on the other side of it.
{name} wraps the coldest survivor in their own jacket without being asked.
{name} won't leave the airlock until every last one is aboard. "Nobody gets left. Not on my watch."
{name} watches the beacon fade off the scope and says nothing. The silence is a kind of answer.
{name}: "...Right. Their problem. Sure." {name} doesn't sleep much that night.
{name}: "Could've been bait. Probably was. Probably."
{name} notices the pay didn't come through. {name} notices everything.
{name}: "We're square when we're square. I've flown for worse than late."
{name}: "I fly for coin, Captain. No coin, no fly. Simple as."
{name} doesn't say anything about the missed pay. That's somehow worse.
{name}'s belt is on a tighter notch than last week. Nobody mentions it.
{name}: "I've been hungrier. Doesn't mean I want to be again, Captain."
{name} stows a single duffel in the bunk and looks around. "So this is home now. Huh."
{name}: "I'm good at what I do. You'll see. Then you'll wonder how you flew without me."
{name} runs a hand along the console like meeting a horse. "We'll get along, you and me."
{name} goes still. "...I know this place. I grew up not far from here."
{name} stares out at the port lights of home and doesn't go down the ramp.
{name} has taken to leaving the radio on at night. "So I'd notice," they say, "if it went quiet."
{name}: "My gran said the black hums if you listen long enough. I always thought she was being poetic."
{name}: "Four seconds. You know what else is four seconds? A breath in and a breath out. Somebody breathing, Captain."
{name} keeps glancing at the comm stack like it owes them money. Nobody talks about the dark worlds at dinner. Everybody thinks about them."
{name}: "If a whole station can go quiet mid-sentence, Captain, I'd like us to have a plan for mid-what, exactly?"
{name}: "The Anechoic. Even the NAME doesn't echo. Whoever charts these things has a sense of humor I don't care for."
{name} hums four seconds of something without noticing, stops, and grins. "It's catchy. Sue me."
{name} turns the radio on, listens to plain static a while, and turns it off. "Just checking," they say. Nobody asks what for.
{name} checks the destination twice before the burn. "Still answering, right? They're still answering?"
{name}: "Dockside radio sounds normal. Normal's worth noticing, these days."
{name} nods at the sealed crate, once, and asks nothing, loudly.
{name}: "That Voss woman doesn't sleep. Light under her door all night. Whatever's in her head weighs more than the cargo."
{name}: "Every hour we're still flying is an hour they haven't caught us. That's the whole scoreboard now, Captain."
{name}: "For the record — whatever happens at that Gate, I'd run this run again. Same crew. Same captain."
{name}: "Sixty thousand people heard the truth because this bucket kept flying. I think about that when the recycler acts up."
{name} still won't sit in the chair Voss used at mess. Nobody's said anything. Nobody's had to.
{name}: "Moving witnesses now, are we. My mother wanted me to have a boring job. Glad she can't see the manifest."
{name}: "They read the names on the newsnets. All sixty thousand. Took four days. We did that, Captain — some of it, anyway."
{name} looks at the glass towers a long moment. "Eleven years ago this month, you know. Whatever the plaque says it was."
{name}: "Meridian Prime. Mind the cameras, mind the tariffs, and don't say 'reactor accident' louder than a whisper."
{name} breathes in the smelter air like perfume. "Cheapest yard in the sector, Captain. If we're fixing anything, fix it here."
{name}: "Port Solace. Everyone passes through, most owing money. Which kind are we today?"
{name}: "Smell that? Actual bread. Buy food here 'til the hold groans, Captain, it's practically free."
{name}: "Haven's Folly. Watch your pockets, mind your manifest, and if a Red Sky man buys you a drink, you already owe him."
{name} cracks their knuckles at the free-port lights. "Now THIS is a proper town."
{name}: "Last light before the black. Everything past here is rumor with coordinates."
{name} stares past the station, out where the stars thin. "Doesn't it pull at you? Just a little?"
{name}: "You run a good ship, Captain. Took me a while to say it. There it is."
{name} tells you the long version of a story from {origin} — the true one, with the embarrassing parts left in. You've been promoted to the real audience.
{name}: "Most captains, you fly FOR. This one's different." They say it to the new hand, not to you. You weren't supposed to hear.
{name} answers in single syllables tonight. The ledger between you has a balance, and it isn't in your favor.
{name} settles into their station like it's the good chair at home. "Wherever we're headed, Captain — aye."
{name}, calm as milk: "We've been through worse with you. Call it."
{name} straps in without a word. You wonder, briefly and not for the first time, which way they'd shoot.
{name} buys the first round dockside and toasts "the only captain worth the paperwork." The regulars drink to you.
{name} develops an urgent fascination with a floor panel while the scan runs.
{name} watches the patrol go with an expression you can't file. Relief? Recognition? It's gone before you can name it.
{name} takes their coffee out to the viewport alone tonight, and stands there a long time, like someone doing arithmetic with a number they won't say.
{name} names the ship on the far scope without looking twice. "Bad crowd," they add, in the tone of someone who used to be invited."
{name} watches the wreck the way you'd watch an old house burn down. "Could've been me flying that. Went another way."
{name} pauses mid-task at a sound in the hull only they heard. "...She's fine," they say, to themselves, and keep working.
{name} folds the Union news feed away half-read, jaw tight, and finds something to polish.
{name} counts their pay twice on payday and sends most of it somewhere they don't name.
{name} walks the ship after the yard crew leaves, touching each trued-up panel. "Now she's got no excuses."
{name} inspects the yard's refit work with narrowed eyes, finds nothing wrong, and is visibly annoyed about it.
{name}: "She's held together with habit and my personal stubbornness, Captain. A refit. I'm begging."
{name}: "Ship's whistling in her sleep. That's not an omen, that's a WORN SEAL, which is worse, because omens are free."
{name} threads gaps that shouldn't fit anymore. "Getting the feel for her," they say, quieter than the first year.
{name}'s aim has stopped looking like luck.
{name} fixes things before you notice they're breaking.
{name}'s bedside manner has sharpened into something patients trust without being told to.
{name} can stretch a thin larder further than the math says is possible.
{name} reads a contract's fine print faster than the broker who wrote it.
{name} can read a debris field like a paragraph now. Nobody in the cockpit doubts the line anymore.
{name} calls shots before they land. Some captains never fly with a gunner this steady.
{name} can jury-rig this ship half-blind. Half the crew think it's a party trick. It isn't.
{name} hasn't lost anyone they got to in time, this many trips in. That's not luck anymore.
Nobody complains about rations anymore. {name} made sure of that, one careful week at a time.
{name} hasn't been out-negotiated in longer than anyone aboard can remember.
